summary: The article argues that database design must evolve alongside application development in agile methodologies, rather than being fixed upfront. Fowler and Sadalagi describe techniques developed over a decade at Thoughtworks for managing database schema changes through small, versioned migration scripts that are treated like application code. The approach treats every database change as a refactoring—combining schema modifications, data migration, and access code changes into atomic units. Key enablers include close DBA-developer collaboration, individual database instances for each developer, continuous integration of database changes, and automation tools. The techniques have scaled to projects with 600+ tables, 100+ developers, and 24/7 uptime requirements.
key_points: Database changes should be captured as versioned migration scripts stored in the same repository as application code, not made ad-hoc through schema editing tools
Every developer should have their own database instance to experiment freely, with automation making it trivial to create, destroy, and rebuild databases
Database refactorings have three dimensions that must change together: schema, data migration, and database access code
Transition phases using views and triggers allow destructive changes to coexist with legacy systems during migration periods
Frequent small integrations are exponentially easier than infrequent large ones—the pain of integration increases exponentially with size
money_quotes: On an agile project you assume that you cannot fix the requirements of the system up-front.

Instead of thinking of design as a phase, which is mostly completed before you begin construction, you look at design as an on-going process that is interleaved with construction, testing, and even delivery.
Database environments should be phoenixes—regularly burnt down and rebuilt at will.
The pain of integration increases exponentially with the size of the integration, so doing many small changes is much easier in practice, even though it appears counter-intuitive to many.
Being schemaless doesn't free us from worrying about schemas, there is still an implicit schema—one that's implied by any code that accesses the database.
If you are determined to automate every task, you can handle lot of work with much less people.
key_insight: Database schema evolution becomes manageable when every change is captured as a small, versioned migration script that travels through the same continuous integration pipeline as application code.
